reviews.summary: This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.
reviews.weaknesses: The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.
reviews.questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.
reviews.limitations: Yes

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.

Strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.

Weaknesses:
The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.

Questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.

Yes

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper proposes CP-SLAM, a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM framework built on a novel neural point representation. By anchoring learnable features to 3D points associated with keyframes (inspired by PointNeRF [43]), CP-SLAM supports complete front-end and back-end SLAM functionalities—dense odometry, loop detection via NetVLAD, sub-map fusion, distributed-to-centralized MLP learning, and global pose graph optimization followed by map refinement. The authors evaluate single- and two-agent scenarios on Replica and TUM datasets, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in both camera tracking (ATE RMSE, Mean, Median) and dense mapping (depth L1, mesh accuracy).

In particular, the distributed-to-centralized two‐stage learning strategy—where each agent initially trains its own MLPs for feature decoding, then federates weights after sub-map merging—reflects careful system design to achieve consistency across agents. Moreover, the keyframe-centric neural point field allows efficient backpropagation of both features (via MLPs C, U, G) and, after loop closure, repositioning of points under refined poses. Extensive ablations (sampling density, point filtering cell size ρ, presence/absence of pose graph optimization) further support the system’s design choices.

Strengths:
1. Novel neural point representation in SLAM.  
   • The paper clearly motivates why point-anchored features—each interpolated from K=8 neighbors within radius r=0.15 m—are more flexible than dense or sparse grids for loop closures and sub-map merging (Fig. 2).  
   • This representation naturally permits rigid alignment under pose graph updates, unlike grid-based implicit fields that misalign when re-cubed into a global frame.

2. Full SLAM pipeline with loop closing and PGO.  
   • CP-SLAM integrates a learning-based loop detection module (NetVLAD descriptors with thresholds λ_coarse, λ_fine) and a back-end global pose graph optimization (Eq. 12).  
   • After PGO, the keyframe-centric design allows a low-cost global map refinement by fine-tuning the neural decoders on all adjusted keyframes, effectively mimicking traditional bundle adjustment.

3. Well-engineered system architecture.  
   • The authors combine differentiable volume rendering (N_near=16, N_uni=4), depth-guided sampling, and separate MLPs (C, U, G) per agent in the distributed stage, followed by federated averaging and fine-tuning.  
   • The implementation details—GPU friendlier FRNN radius search, periodic map optimization every 10 frames, and a practical neural point filtering via ρ³ non-maximum suppression cubes—demonstrate excellent engineering rigor.

4. Competitive quantitative and qualitative results.  
   • In two-agent experiments on Replica Apartment-0/1/2 and Office-0-C, CP-SLAM outperforms CCM-SLAM [33], Swarm-SLAM [17], and ORB-SLAM3 [4] in ATE RMSE, Mean, Median (Table 1, Fig. 5).  
   • In single-agent loop scenarios, it surpasses NICE-SLAM [50] and Vox-Fusion [44] by 10% on average ATE (Table 2) and achieves lower depth L1 and higher mesh accuracy (Table 4, Fig. 7).  
   • On TUM-RGBD, it also yields the best real-world tracking performance compared to Co-SLAM [39] and ESLAM [13,12] (Table 3).

Weaknesses:
From a novelty standpoint, many individual components—point-based fields (PointNeRF), NetVLAD loop detection, federated learning, pose graph optimization—are incremental adaptations of existing techniques. The system as a whole is well integrated, but the core ideas derive from prior work with mainly engineering-driven modifications rather than fundamentally new algorithms.

Questions:
1. In Sec. 3.1, it appears that at each frame a dense set of new neural points is back-projected, without point merging. How does CP-SLAM maintain only 0.62 MB of feature parameters (Tab. 6) given potentially unbounded point growth?  
2. Also in Sec. 3.1, the rendering loss optimizes per-point features via MLPs but not point locations. Does this imply that point coordinates remain fixed until loop detection/PGO? If so, is mapping simply storing back-projected depth points without geometry refinement?  
3. How is the point-based map converted to a mesh for evaluation in Table 4? What TSDF fusion settings (e.g., voxel size, truncation) were used?  
4. Since CP-SLAM’s map points originate directly from depth measurements, one might expect inherently higher accuracy than purely learned methods (similar to TSDF fusion). Can the authors clarify why improved reconstruction metrics definitively reflect “more powerful geometry reconstruction capability” rather than the advantage of explicit depth back-projection? Also, why was reconstruction completion not reported?

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.

Limitations:
Yes

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: The paper presents a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system for RGB-D image sequences, which is able to merge sub-maps into a global map and also handle loop-closure for neural mapper for the first time. To facilitate the capability of doing loop-closure and sub-map merging, the authors introduce a neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a keyframe. The optimization follows the traditional SLAM’s graph optimization fashion. The experimental results on diverse datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.
reviews.strengths:
1. Novel Neural Point-based 3D Scene Representation: A neural point cloud representation for scene encoding in the proposed framework is a significant contribution. This approach allows each point to carry encoded information, improving the system's ability to represent and cross-view association for large-scale tracking and pose refinement. 
2.Comprehensive Collaborative Framework: The authors provide a collaborative SLAM system with front-end and back-end modules, addressing essential aspects such as odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. This comprehensive approach ensures that the proposed system can handle various challenges typically encountered in SLAM tasks. 
3.Distributed-to-Centralized Learning Strategy: The incorporation of a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is an effective solution for improving consistency and cooperation in collaborative implicit SLAM. 
4.Improved Performance: The experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.
reviews.weaknesses:
1.In the Introduction: This paper emphasizes the significance of targeting collaborative-SLAM, considering the substantial value already provided by loop-closure capability in effectively reducing pose drift. However, the evaluation of the paper becomes more challenging due to the necessity for additional comparative studies that can convincingly demonstrate the advantages offered by both approaches.

2.In Section 2: For collaborative mapping, this paper should cite more recent works including:
[1*] Campos, Carlos, et al. "Orb-slam3: An accurate open-source library for visual, visual–inertial, and multimap slam." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37.6 (2021): 1874-1890.
[2*] Tian, Yulun, et al. "Kimera-multi: Robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic slam for multi-robot systems." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 38.4 (2022).

It is also expected to provide comparative studies to the above researches.

3.In Section 3: 
(1). Line 101: This paper introduced 4x4 patches to compute the point features. However, it misses details on there is a limitation on the number of patches. What if we increase the size of the patches? Will it impact the later performance? 
(2) This paper introduced so many symbols but without giving any explanation of the symbols, which makes it really hard to follow the paper. Please revise the whole paper and make them clear to the readers.
(3) Equation 10: The explanation “Considering the strong non-convexity of the color map, only the geometry part is included in tracking loss” for not using color in the loss is not convincing. The authors are expected to provide ablation studies to prove the claim.

4.In Section 4: More experiments are expected:
(1). More comparative studies on Collaborative-SLAM are expected.
(2). For single-agent cases: The local covisibility map can be formed as well; it would be interesting to only look at cases without a loop but having a local covisibility map.
(3). Ablation study on “Distributed-to-Centralized Learning” is needed to prove why this is needed.
reviews.questions:
1.In the context of this paper, which relies on point features for establishing frame-to-frame correspondence, an important concern arises regarding the maintenance of continuous tracking. Unlike the traditional SLAM pipeline, where feature locations typically reside around corners, this paper selects points without necessarily adhering to this characteristic. Thus, it becomes crucial to address how this approach ensures the continuity of tracking throughout the SLAM process.
2.Collaborative mapping can be done by recent work on No Pose Nerf:
[]Bian, Wenjing, et al. "Nope-nerf: Optimising neural radiance field with no pose prior." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2023.
This paper should provide a comparison to show the advantage of the proposed method.
3. This paper has too many contributions, which does not form a scientific paper but more an engineering paper. Not sure if this paper fall into the NeuralIPS group.
reviews.limitations:
1. The writing should be improved, especially the equations should be better explained.
2. More experiments and ablation studies should be provided to demonstrate the advantage of the proposed algorithms. 
3. The literature review needs to refer to more recent research.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper presents a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system for RGB-D image sequences, which is able to merge sub-maps into a global map and also handle loop-closure for neural mapper for the first time. To facilitate the capability of doing loop-closure and sub-map merging, the authors introduce a neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a keyframe. The optimization follows the traditional SLAM’s graph optimization fashion. The experimental results on diverse datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.

Strengths:
1. Novel Neural Point-based 3D Scene Representation: A neural point cloud representation for scene encoding in the proposed framework is a significant contribution. This approach allows each point to carry encoded information, improving the system's ability to represent and cross-view association for large-scale tracking and pose refinement. 
2.Comprehensive Collaborative Framework: The authors provide a collaborative SLAM system with front-end and back-end modules, addressing essential aspects such as odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. This comprehensive approach ensures that the proposed system can handle various challenges typically encountered in SLAM tasks. 
3.Distributed-to-Centralized Learning Strategy: The incorporation of a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is an effective solution for improving consistency and cooperation in collaborative implicit SLAM. 
4.Improved Performance: The experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.

Weaknesses:
1.In the Introduction: This paper emphasizes the significance of targeting collaborative-SLAM, considering the substantial value already provided by loop-closure capability in effectively reducing pose drift. However, the evaluation of the paper becomes more challenging due to the necessity for additional comparative studies that can convincingly demonstrate the advantages offered by both approaches.

2.In Section 2: For collaborative mapping, this paper should cite more recent works including:
[1*] Campos, Carlos, et al. "Orb-slam3: An accurate open-source library for visual, visual–inertial, and multimap slam." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37.6 (2021): 1874-1890.
[2*] Tian, Yulun, et al. "Kimera-multi: Robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic slam for multi-robot systems." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 38.4 (2022).

It is also expected to provide comparative studies to the above researches.

3.In Section 3: 
(1). Line 101: This paper introduced 4x4 patches to compute the point features. However, it misses details on there is a limitation on the number of patches. What if we increase the size of the patches? Will it impact the later performance? 
(2) This paper introduced so many symbols but without giving any explanation of the symbols, which makes it really hard to follow the paper. Please revise the whole paper and make them clear to the readers.
(3) Equation 10: The explanation “Considering the strong non-convexity of the color map, only the geometry part is included in tracking loss” for not using color in the loss is not convincing. The authors are expected to provide ablation studies to prove the claim.

4.In Section 4: More experiments are expected:
(1). More comparative studies on Collaborative-SLAM are expected.
(2). For single-agent cases: The local covisibility map can be formed as well; it would be interesting to only look at cases without a loop but having a local covisibility map.
(3). Ablation study on “Distributed-to-Centralized Learning” is needed to prove why this is needed.

Questions:
1.In the context of this paper, which relies on point features for establishing frame-to-frame correspondence, an important concern arises regarding the maintenance of continuous tracking. Unlike the traditional SLAM pipeline, where feature locations typically reside around corners, this paper selects points without necessarily adhering to this characteristic. Thus, it becomes crucial to address how this approach ensures the continuity of tracking throughout the SLAM process.
2.Collaborative mapping can be done by recent work on No Pose Nerf:
[]Bian, Wenjing, et al. "Nope-nerf: Optimising neural radiance field with no pose prior." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2023.
This paper should provide a comparison to show the advantage of the proposed method.
3. This paper has too many contributions, which does not form a scientific paper but more an engineering paper. Not sure if this paper fall into the NeuralIPS group.

Limitations:
1. The writing should be improved, especially the equations should be better explained.
2. More experiments and ablation studies should be provided to demonstrate the advantage of the proposed algorithms. 
3. The literature review needs to refer to more recent research.

1. The writing should be improved, especially the equations should be better explained.
2. More experiments and ablation studies should be provided to demonstrate the advantage of the proposed algorithms. 
3. The literature review needs to refer to more recent research.

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.
reviews.strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.
reviews.weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.
reviews.questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.
reviews.limitations: Limitations are adequately addressed.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.

Strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.

Weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.

Questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.

Limitations:
Limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Learning Repeatable Speech Embeddings Using An Intra-class Correlation Regularizer

reviews.summary: Starting with the observation that supervised embeddings should vary from one class to another but not be sensitive to variations within a given class, this paper proposes to add an intra-class correlation (ICC) regularization to the contrastive loss in representation learning. This new regularizer is derived from measurement theory and encourages the embeddings to have a low intra-class variance, whereas contrastive losses pushes more the embeddings towards high inter-class variances. After a simulation illustrating the "behaviour" of these losses with respect to inter- and intra-class variance, the authors report good improvements using the proposed ICC regularizer for three different tasks.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper is overall well written, the idea well explained and easy to follow and to understand
  - The ICC regularizer seems to really focus on intra-class variance, i.e. make all occurences of the same class have the same embeddings, where contrastive loss does both at the same time and never really focuses on minimizing the intra-class variance.
  - The method is relatively simple, very well explained and easy to understand. The simulation, comparison with contrastive loss and classification error rate is quite interesting.
  - When the embeddings are constrained, it is indeed useful to focus on minimizing the intra-class variance to separate classes. The explanation and results are convincing.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Missing comparison with other techniques mentioned in the related work (data augmentation, etc.). Is the proposed approach competitive with these methods? Could it be complementary? How does it compare to VICReg [4] for example
  - In the result section, the text mainly lists the results in the tables. Maybe a reformulation would allow more space for example to study the effect of the regularization weight
reviews.questions:
Could that method apply to self-supervised approaches, where the notion of classes is less well defined?

Is it competitive with other methods mentioned at the beginning of the paper? Or could it be complementary?

Are the improvements with that method bigger on smaller datasets or are they independent of the dataset size? Is the method especially suited for some tasks and less for other (e.g. could that be useful for classification, or ASR?)?

minor remark:
  - sec 3.1: "represents the i-th samples" -> sample
reviews.limitations: yes
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

  - The ICC regularizer seems to really focus on intra-class variance, i.e. make all occurences of the same class have the same embeddings, where contrastive loss does both at the same time and never really focuses on minimizing the intra-class variance.

reviews.text:
Review for “Learning Repeatable Speech Embeddings Using An Intra-class Correlation Regularizer”

Summary:
This paper introduces a novel regularizer based on the intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) to explicitly enforce repeatability in learned embeddings. The authors begin with the observation—rooted in measurement theory—that a desirable supervised embedding should exhibit low intra-class variance (i.e., be repeatable across samples of the same class) while retaining sufficient inter-class separation. Existing contrastive losses, like GE2E, simultaneously push for both low intra-class variance and high inter-class variance, but never focus exclusively on minimizing intra-class variance. To address this, the paper (1) proposes using ICC as a repeatability metric, (2) formulates an ICC-based regularizer that complements standard contrastive losses, and (3) offers an analytical simulation (8-dimensional, 4-class Gaussian mixtures) illustrating how the ICC regularizer emphasizes intra-class variance reduction, contrasting with GE2E’s simultaneous push on both variances. Empirically, the ICC regularizer is applied to three speech tasks—text-independent speaker verification (TI-SV) on VoxCeleb, zero-shot voice style conversion on VCTK, and dysphonic voice detection across multiple clinical corpora—and yields consistent improvements. For TI-SV, adding the ICC term reduces EER by approximately 10% (e.g., from 4.39% to 3.96% using VGG-M-40) and increases mean ICC score from 0.4494 to 0.5487. In zero-shot voice conversion, an AB preference test over 100 speaker pairs shows a 62% preference for ICC-regularized embeddings, and objective metrics (WER, CER, speaker similarity) also improve. In dysphonic voice detection, embeddings with higher repeatability boost cross-corpus balanced accuracy by up to ∼3% and ICC from 0.4368 to 0.5708. The code is publicly available for reproducibility.

Strengths:
1. Clarity and Exposition:
   - The paper is very well written and logically structured. Key definitions—such as repeatability borrowed from measurement theory, the ICC1-1 formulation, and the distinction between intra- and inter-class variances—are clearly presented in Section 3.1.
   - The authors provide both intuition and formal derivations for the ICC regularizer, including gradient expressions (Equations 7 and 8) that explain why it focuses more on intra-class variance when the inter-class variance is already large.
   - Figures are informative: Figure 1 contrasts the contour landscapes of GE2E loss, ICC regularizer, and SVM classification error, effectively illustrating how the ICC term aligns better with downstream error rates.

2. Novelty and Theoretical Insight:
   - Leveraging the well-established ICC from measurement theory in representation learning is novel. To my knowledge, this is the first work to both evaluate and regularize embeddings using ICC.
   - The simulation study using 8-dimensional, 4-class Gaussian mixtures with controlled intra-/inter-class variances is elegant. It clearly demonstrates that GE2E loss continues to push for larger inter-class variance even when clusters are well separated, while the ICC regularizer focuses on further reducing intra-class variance.

3. Empirical Validation:
   - The ICC regularizer is validated on three disparate and challenging speech tasks, covering both speaker identity (TI-SV, zero-shot conversion) and clinical voice quality (dysphonic detection). This breadth strengthens the generality claim.
   - Quantitative gains are substantial and consistent. For TI-SV on VoxCeleb1, all three contrastive baselines (GE2E, Angular Prototypical, SupCon) see relative EER reductions of 3–10% when augmented with ICC regularization; ICC scores for embeddings also rise markedly.
   - In zero-shot voice style conversion, the subjective AB test (62% vs. 38% preference) and objective metrics provide convergent evidence that higher-repeatability embeddings yield better voice transfer.
   - In the clinical task, the proposed method attains the highest repeatability (ICC = 0.5708 on the unseen ALS dataset) and shows improved balanced accuracy in both in-corpus (SVD) and cross-corpus (MEEI, HUPA) settings, outperforming four strong baselines.

4. Reproducibility and Resources:
   - The authors release their code and detailed appendix covering the ICC extension for imbalanced classes, hyperparameter ablations (Appendix B), model architectures (Appendix C), and dataset summaries (Appendix D). This transparency is commendable.

Weaknesses:
- Comparison to Related Invariance Methods:
  The related work section mentions data augmentation strategies and other regularizers, such as VICReg [4], that also aim to reduce unwanted variance. However, the empirical comparisons do not include these methods. It would strengthen the paper to evaluate how the ICC regularizer performs relative to, or in combination with, data augmentation approaches or VICReg. Could ICC be complementary to variance-invariance-covariance regularization?
- Analysis of Regularization Weight:
  In the experimental section (e.g., Table 1), the text mainly lists EER and ICC improvements for fixed λ values. A more detailed study of how varying the ICC regularization weight λ impacts both repeatability and downstream performance (beyond the simulation in Appendix B) would be useful. For instance, what happens if λ is set too high—does it hurt inter-class separability?
- Computational Overhead:
  The ICC regularizer requires computing variances and means across mini-batches of size N×M and across embedding dimensions. The paper could briefly discuss the additional computational cost during training and how it scales with N, M, and embedding dimension L.
- Minor Clarification:
  In Section 3.1, fix the typo “represents the i-th samples” to “i-th sample.”

Questions for the Authors:
1. Self-Supervised Extension: The ICC regularizer relies on class labels to define intra-class groupings. Could it be adapted to self-supervised or unsupervised frameworks where explicit classes are not available? For example, by using pseudo-labels or clustering?
2. Data Efficiency: Are the benefits of ICC regularization more pronounced on smaller datasets (where intra-class variance might be higher) or do they persist at large scales (e.g., VoxCeleb2)? Have you observed any diminishing returns as dataset size grows?
3. Task Suitability: Beyond speaker verification, conversion, and dysphonic detection, do you anticipate this regularizer being beneficial for other tasks such as ASR (where phoneme classes might be the grouping) or image classification?

Decision:
7: Accept.  
This paper presents a technically solid and novel contribution—importing ICC from measurement theory into deep representation learning—as both an evaluation metric and a training regularizer. The idea is clearly motivated, well analyzed theoretically and empirically, and validated across multiple realistic speech tasks with consistent performance gains. While comparisons to other invariance-promoting methods would further strengthen the work, the current submission already offers significant insights and practical impact for embedding-based systems. The code release further ensures reproducibility. I recommend acceptance.

The paper presents a simple but seemingly effective idea to regularize contrastive embedding extractors. The regularizer aims at the intra-class correlation to ensure the repeatability of the embedding.
The effectiveness is confirmed experimentally for the task of speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection.

Strengths:
The presented idea is well introduced and the illustration of the ICC regularizer action compared to the GE2E loss is insightful. The evaluation is sufficient.

Weaknesses:

Questions:

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Expanded Review

Summary:  
This paper introduces a novel intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complementary term to standard contrastive losses for training deep embeddings. By explicitly minimizing the intra-class variance (and thus maximizing repeatability) rather than solely focusing on inter-class separation, the proposed ICC regularizer encourages embeddings to be robust to confounding factors such as noise, session variation, and day-to-day physiological changes. The authors provide a clear derivation of the ICC loss (Equation 6 in the manuscript), simulate its effect on intra- and inter-class variance landscapes (Figure 1), and demonstrate its practical benefits on three speech-related tasks: text-independent speaker verification (TI-SV), zero-shot voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection. Across these applications, adding the ICC regularizer consistently yields measurable improvements in repeatability metrics (ICC scores) and downstream performance (EER reduction, AB preference ratios, classification accuracy). The code is publicly available, facilitating reproducibility.

Strengths:  
1. Clarity of exposition: The authors lay out the mathematical formulation of the ICC 1–1 estimator (Equations 2–5), and show how it can be converted into a differentiable loss term (Equation 6). This step-by-step presentation makes it easy for readers to understand and implement the method.  
2. Insightful comparison with contrastive losses: The contour plots in Figure 1(a–c) vividly illustrate why standard contrastive objectives (e.g., GE2E) may struggle to further reduce intra-class variance once inter-class separation is sufficient, whereas the ICC regularizer naturally prioritizes driving intra-class variance toward zero. The accompanying gradient analysis (Equations 7–8) provides an intuitive, quantitative explanation of this behavior.  
3. Comprehensive empirical evaluation: The paper evaluates the ICC regularizer on three distinct speech tasks. In TI-SV experiments (Table 1), adding ICC to GE2E, Angular Prototypical, and SupCon losses yields 3–10% relative reductions in Equal Error Rate (EER) and consistent increases in embedding ICC scores. For zero-shot voice conversion (Tables 2–3), embeddings trained with ICC receive 62% AB-test preference over baseline and show improved speaker similarity, WER, and CER. In dysphonic voice detection (Table 4), training a two-branch model with ICC increases cross-corpus balanced accuracy by up to ∼3% and boosts embedding repeatability from 0.4368 to 0.5708 ICC. These results demonstrate the broad applicability and robustness of the approach.  
4. Reproducibility and practical guidance: The authors release code on GitHub and include detailed hyperparameter settings, data splits (e.g., VoxCeleb1/2, VCTK, SVD, MEEI, HUPA, mPower, ALS), and training details (batch sizes, λ values, optimizers). They also discuss how to extend the ICC formulation to imbalanced class scenarios in Appendix A, aiding practical adoption.

Weaknesses:  
• Dependence on second-order statistics: The ICC regularizer relies exclusively on second-order moments—inter-class and intra-class variances—to quantify repeatability. While variance captures many forms of measurement consistency, it ignores higher-order distributional characteristics (e.g., skewness, multimodality) that might also affect embedding quality. In domains where intra-class distributions are strongly non-Gaussian, an ICC-based loss may not fully characterize or correct failure modes such as outlier clusters or asymmetric noise. On the other hand, second-order statistics remain computationally efficient to estimate in minibatches and are sufficient for many practical scenarios, so this limitation may not be critical in most applications.  

Questions:  
• Relation to mutual information: Contrastive losses have been linked to lower bounds on mutual information between augmentations or class labels and embeddings. Since mutual information measures joint and marginal distributions, one might expect that an optimal mutual information‐maximizing embedding would also exhibit high ICC (i.e., low intra-class variance). In practice, however, the contrastive objectives used (e.g., GE2E) appear to trade off variance terms in a way that does not fully prioritize intra-class consistency, as shown by the simulation study. Could the authors clarify whether, under Gaussian assumptions, maximizing mutual information between labels and embeddings would indeed correspond to an ICC‐centric objective, or whether the parametric forms of GE2E and related losses inherently decouple the two? Further, would incorporating ICC into a mutual information estimator yield additional gains beyond the pure ICC regularization?  

Limitations:  
• Second‐order only: As noted, the proposed regularizer is confined to second-order statistics. It does not penalize, for instance, differences in skewness or higher-order moments across embedding dimensions. Extending the framework to capture richer forms of repeatability (e.g., using higher-order cumulants or Wasserstein distances) remains an open direction.  
• Hyperparameter sensitivity: The balance weight λ between contrastive loss and ICC regularizer requires tuning (as discussed in Section 3.2 and Appendix B). While the authors provide guidance and ablation results for λ on simulated data, real-world deployments may need additional grid searches, especially when class size M varies or when embedding dimension L is large.  
• Class‐size requirements: The ICC formulation in the main paper assumes balanced batch construction (equal M per class). Although Appendix A describes an extension for imbalanced classes, the computational cost and variance of ICC estimation may increase when class counts differ widely within minibatches. Practical guidelines on batch formation for stability would strengthen the contribution.

Decision: Weak Accept (Rating = 6)  
This paper presents a technically sound and well‐motivated approach for improving the repeatability of learned embeddings by harnessing a classic measurement theory metric. The theoretical insights, coupled with extensive evaluations across speech tasks, support its moderate‐to‐high impact. There are no major flaws in the evaluation methodology, resources, or ethical considerations, and the code release ensures reproducibility. I recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary: In this paper, authors propose to use an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complimentary component for contrastive loss to increase the repeatability of the supervised speech embedding. Authors conduct simulation for ICC regularizer and contrastive loss (GE2E loss) to explain their similarities and differences in the optimization criteria. Based on the experimental results on the speaker verification, voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic three tasks, the authors claim that adding an ICC regularizer helps increase the repeatability and further improve the performance on downstream tasks.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper leverages the concept of repeatability and states that higher repeatability can contribute to improved performance in downstream tasks. Authors think about the importance of intra-class variance and the proposed ICC regularizer places greater emphasis on minimizing it to help increase repeatability. The simulation results in Section 3.2 explains the intuition clearly. 
 
2. As a complementary component to contrastive loss, adding the proposed ICC regularizer successfully increase the repeatability and shows good performance in the three different tasks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. In Task 1, the performance of SV system is not good though adding the ICC regularizer improves its performance. In this paper, VGG-M-40 is trained on VoxCeleb1 & 2. The best result (3.78% EER) reported in Table 1 is not as good as other commonly used models for SV. In Chung et al. paper, the VGG-M-40 shows much worse performance than Thin-ResNet34 and Fast ResNet34. To make the experimental solid and results and claims more convincible, authors should pick a good baseline instead of use the VGG-M-40.     

2. For better replicable, more experiment details should be provided, such as the training details of Task 1.     

3. To have comprehensive evaluation results for Task 1, the evaluation metric minDCF can be used as well.

4. Visualization (such as t-SNE) can be added as a comparison to see if the extracted speech or speaker embeddings have smaller intra-class variance in the space, though their ICC is decreased.
reviews.questions:
1. As this paper mentioned, the ICC regularizer requires hyperparameter fine-tuning. Are the hyperparameter tuned on a validation set or a development set? 

2. As this paper mentioned in Section 3.2, though the ICC increased, do the learned embeddings are more compacted within-class and more separate between-class in the overall embedding space? Authors can use t-SNE to visualize them.
reviews.limitations: The authors do not analyze the potential negative societal impact.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, authors propose to use an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complimentary component for contrastive loss to increase the repeatability of the supervised speech embedding. Authors conduct simulation for ICC regularizer and contrastive loss (GE2E loss) to explain their similarities and differences in the optimization criteria. Based on the experimental results on the speaker verification, voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic three tasks, the authors claim that adding an ICC regularizer helps increase the repeatability and further improve the performance on downstream tasks.

Strengths:
1. This paper leverages the concept of repeatability and states that higher repeatability can contribute to improved performance in downstream tasks. Authors think about the importance of intra-class variance and the proposed ICC regularizer places greater emphasis on minimizing it to help increase repeatability. The simulation results in Section 3.2 explains the intuition clearly. 
 
2. As a complementary component to contrastive loss, adding the proposed ICC regularizer successfully increase the repeatability and shows good performance in the three different tasks.

Weaknesses:
1. In Task 1, the performance of SV system is not good though adding the ICC regularizer improves its performance. In this paper, VGG-M-40 is trained on VoxCeleb1 & 2. The best result (3.78% EER) reported in Table 1 is not as good as other commonly used models for SV. In Chung et al. paper, the VGG-M-40 shows much worse performance than Thin-ResNet34 and Fast ResNet34. To make the experimental solid and results and claims more convincible, authors should pick a good baseline instead of use the VGG-M-40.     

2. For better replicable, more experiment details should be provided, such as the training details of Task 1.     

3. To have comprehensive evaluation results for Task 1, the evaluation metric minDCF can be used as well.

4. Visualization (such as t-SNE) can be added as a comparison to see if the extracted speech or speaker embeddings have smaller intra-class variance in the space, though their ICC is decreased.

Questions:
1. As this paper mentioned, the ICC regularizer requires hyperparameter fine-tuning. Are the hyperparameter tuned on a validation set or a development set? 

2. As this paper mentioned in Section 3.2, though the ICC increased, do the learned embeddings are more compacted within-class and more separate between-class in the overall embedding space? Authors can use t-SNE to visualize them.

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze the potential negative societal impact.

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
In this paper, the concept of repeatability from measurement theory was introduced for representation learning. Intraclass Correlation (ICC) was proposed as an evaluation metric and the ICC regularizer was used as an additional element to contrastive loss for training. This aims to enhance the repeatability of deep-learned embeddings.
Some synthetic examples and intuitive reasoning were used to illustrate why the ICC regularizer is superior in minimizing intra-class variance compared to contrastive loss. The ICC regularizer was assessed across three speech tasks: speaker embeddings for Text-Independent Speaker Verification (TI-SV) and zero-shot voice style conversion, along with voice feature embeddings for a clinical application. The experiments indicate that the ICC regularizer can boost the repeatability of learned embeddings and perform better in downstream tasks.
reviews.strengths:
The paper proposed a novel metric of ICC based on measurement repeatability. Although the term may be a little confusing, the motivation 
and intuition were well explained. The experimental results on three speaker/speech tasks are convincing.
reviews.weaknesses:
To show the repeatability of speaker embedding for TI-SV task, more controlled experiments may be necessary to prove the effectiveness of ICC loss in addition to the ICC scores: for example, adding noise to the testing sample, or switching languages of the same speaker as it was tested in the NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation. 

For results on dysphonic voice detection in Table 3, other baseline models were definitely over-trained compared to the proposed model.
Why do you train your own models instead of quoting the numbers from the original papers? (see line 334-335). The results in Table 3 may be unfair comparisons.
reviews.questions: See the comments above in the weakness section.
reviews.limitations: No discussion on negative societal impact was included in the paper

Summary:
In this paper, the concept of repeatability from measurement theory was introduced for representation learning. Intraclass Correlation (ICC) was proposed as an evaluation metric and the ICC regularizer was used as an additional element to contrastive loss for training. This aims to enhance the repeatability of deep-learned embeddings.
Some synthetic examples and intuitive reasoning were used to illustrate why the ICC regularizer is superior in minimizing intra-class variance compared to contrastive loss. The ICC regularizer was assessed across three speech tasks: speaker embeddings for Text-Independent Speaker Verification (TI-SV) and zero-shot voice style conversion, along with voice feature embeddings for a clinical application. The experiments indicate that the ICC regularizer can boost the repeatability of learned embeddings and perform better in downstream tasks.

Strengths:
The paper proposed a novel metric of ICC based on measurement repeatability. Although the term may be a little confusing, the motivation 
and intuition were well explained. The experimental results on three speaker/speech tasks are convincing.

Weaknesses:
To show the repeatability of speaker embedding for TI-SV task, more controlled experiments may be necessary to prove the effectiveness of ICC loss in addition to the ICC scores: for example, adding noise to the testing sample, or switching languages of the same speaker as it was tested in the NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation.

reviews.summary: The paper introduces the ICC regularizer, a novel regularization technique that complements the contrastive loss to enhance the repeatability of embeddings. The authors illustrate the reason why the ICC regularizer better minimizes the intra-class variance than the contrastive loss, offering a fresh perspective on latent representation learning. The experimental results demonstrate that the ICC regularizer enhances the repeatability of learned embeddings. Consequently, embeddings with higher repeatability exhibit superior performance in downstream tasks.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper links the concept of repeatability, derived from measurement theory, to deep-learned embeddings, which is novel and interesting. The authors present an explanation of how the ICC regularizer effectively reduces intra-class variance compared to the contrastive loss, offering a novel perspective on latent representation learning.
2. To enhance the repeatability of the learned embeddings, this paper introduces a novel regularizer called the ICC regularizer, which is used as a regularizer for the contrastive loss.
3. The experimental results indicate that using the ICC regularizer leading to better performance in three downstream tasks .
reviews.weaknesses: My main concerns are mainly related to the experiments. Please refer to the following Questions section for details.
reviews.questions:
1. In Section 4.2, ''GE2E Loss + ICC Regularizer'' shows superior speech naturalness on subjective evaluations. However, the objective metrics like speaker similarity scores and word error rate (WER) are missing.
2. In Section 4.2, the paper aims at zero-shot voice style conversion, which requires highly generalized speaker embedding. However, the authors train and evaluate the zero-shot voice style conversion on a small dataset called VCTK. I would like to ask if the generalization performance of the proposed method be further improved by using larger training datasets? Additional experiments on larger dataset would make the paper more convincing.
3. Although the audio samples with ICC regularizer is better, after listening to the audio samples in the supplementary materials, I think the audio quality should be further improved for practical scenarios. I would like to ask is this due to the used AutoVC model? Could the authors use the latest SOTA voice conversion model to improve the speech quality?

I would like to raise my scores if the authors have fully addressed my concerns.
reviews.limitations: Some limitations of the work are discussed. The potential negative societal impact of the work is not addressed.

Summary:
The paper introduces the ICC regularizer, a novel regularization technique that complements the contrastive loss to enhance the repeatability of embeddings. The authors illustrate the reason why the ICC regularizer better minimizes the intra-class variance than the contrastive loss, offering a fresh perspective on latent representation learning. The experimental results demonstrate that the ICC regularizer enhances the repeatability of learned embeddings. Consequently, embeddings with higher repeatability exhibit superior performance in downstream tasks.

Strengths:
1. This paper links the concept of repeatability, derived from measurement theory, to deep-learned embeddings, which is novel and interesting. The authors present an explanation of how the ICC regularizer effectively reduces intra-class variance compared to the contrastive loss, offering a novel perspective on latent representation learning.
2. To enhance the repeatability of the learned embeddings, this paper introduces a novel regularizer called the ICC regularizer, which is used as a regularizer for the contrastive loss.
3. The experimental results indicate that using the ICC regularizer leading to better performance in three downstream tasks .

Weaknesses:
My main concerns are mainly related to the experiments. Please refer to the following Questions section for details.

Questions:
1. In Section 4.2, ''GE2E Loss + ICC Regularizer'' shows superior speech naturalness on subjective evaluations. However, the objective metrics like speaker similarity scores and word error rate (WER) are missing.
2. In Section 4.2, the paper aims at zero-shot voice style conversion, which requires highly generalized speaker embedding. However, the authors train and evaluate the zero-shot voice style conversion on a small dataset called VCTK. I would like to ask if the generalization performance of the proposed method be further improved by using larger training datasets? Additional experiments on larger dataset would make the paper more convincing.
3. Although the audio samples with ICC regularizer is better, after listening to the audio samples in the supplementary materials, I think the audio quality should be further improved for practical scenarios. I would like to ask is this due to the used AutoVC model? Could the authors use the latest SOTA voice conversion model to improve the speech quality?

I would like to raise my scores if the authors have fully addressed my concerns.

Limitations:
Some limitations of the work are discussed. The potential negative societal impact of the work is not addressed.

reviews.text:
Expanded Review

Summary:  
This paper proposes an intra-class correlation (ICC) regularizer to explicitly enforce embedding repeatability in deep networks trained with contrastive-style losses. Building on measurement theory, the authors introduce the ICC as a principled metric for repeatability (Section 3.1) and design a differentiable regularizer (Equation 6) that complements standard contrastive objectives such as GE2E. A detailed Monte Carlo simulation in Section 3.2 (Figure 1) illustrates how ICC loss contours prioritize minimizing intra-class variance under bounded total variance—offering an intuitive explanation for why contrastive losses alone often underperform on intra-class scatter. The method is then evaluated on three speech applications: text-independent speaker verification (TI-SV) (Table 1), zero-shot voice style conversion (Tables 2–3), and dysphonic voice detection (Table 4). In each case, adding the ICC regularizer yields more repeatable embeddings (higher ICC scores) and corresponding gains in target metrics (EER, AB preference, classification accuracy). The authors also release their code for reproducibility.

Strengths:  
1. Novel theoretical connection. The paper elegantly borrows the ICC from classical measurement theory to define and quantify embedding repeatability. This conceptual bridge is original in the deep-learning literature. The derivation of the ICC formulation (Equations 2–5) and its extension to imbalanced classes (Appendix A) are clearly presented, offering a new lens for thinking about feature invariance.  
2. Innovative regularizer design. The ICC regularizer is a simple, differentiable term (Equation 6) that can be plugged into any contrastive loss. Its implementation per mini-batch (Section 3.1) and the hyperparameter study in Appendix B demonstrate how to balance intra- and inter-class variance through λ. The authors thoughtfully discuss why ICC cannot replace—but rather complements—contrastive losses in Section 3.2.  
3. Strong empirical validation. The method is tested on three diverse speech tasks, each with quantitative and qualitative assessments:  
   – Speaker verification: Adding ICC to GE2E reduces EER by ∼10% on VoxCeleb1 with VGG-M-40 and yields ICC improvements from 0.2925 to 0.5487 (Table 1).  
   – Voice conversion: In a zero-shot AutoVC setup, embeddings trained with ICC are preferred 62%–38% in AB tests (Table 2) and yield higher speaker similarity scores and lower ASR WER/CER (Table 3).  
   – Dysphonic detection: A two-branch model incorporating ICC outperforms four baselines in cross-corpus balanced accuracy and raises embedding ICC from 0.4368 to 0.5708 (Table 4).  
The combination of simulation, ablation, and real-world experiments provides a comprehensive picture of the ICC regularizer’s benefits.

Weaknesses:  
My main concerns are primarily related to the experimental evaluation. While the proposed work is technically solid, important details remain unaddressed in certain sections, leading to questions about the thoroughness and generality of the findings. Please see below for specific questions.

Questions:  
1. In Section 4.2, the authors report subjective AB preference results showing improved naturalness with “GE2E Loss + ICC Regularizer.” However, they do not present objective metrics such as speaker similarity scores or word error rate (WER) in the main text. Although Table 3 later lists similarity and WER/CER, it would strengthen the paper to move or summarize these objective results into Section 4.2’s discussion.  
2. The zero-shot voice style conversion experiments rely solely on the VCTK corpus (Section 4.2). VCTK is relatively small (109 speakers) compared to modern large-scale voice-conversion benchmarks. Would training on a larger, more diverse dataset (e.g., VoxCeleb2 combined with LibriSpeech speakers) improve the generality of the ICC-enhanced embeddings? Additional experiments on larger, multilingual, or more challenging datasets would bolster the claim that ICC regularization universally enhances zero-shot conversion.  
3. The listening samples in the supplementary materials show that the converted speech still exhibits occasional artifacts and limited intelligibility in practical scenarios. Is this limitation attributable to using the AutoVC architecture rather than a more recent state-of-the-art conversion model? It would be valuable to see whether replacing AutoVC with stronger architectures (e.g., your own self-attention-based model or a diffusion-based converter) preserves the ICC regularizer’s benefits while improving raw audio quality.

Addressing these questions by adding objective metrics inline, expanding to larger conversion corpora, or testing alternative conversion backbones would make the evaluation more convincing. I would be inclined to raise my score if these concerns are fully addressed.

Limitations:  
The authors briefly mention limitations and future directions in the Conclusion (applying ICC to vision/NLP, self-supervised learning, deeper theoretical analysis). However, the Potential Negative Societal Impact section only notes bias amplification in feature learning. A more detailed discussion of how ICC regularization interacts with dataset bias—especially in high-stakes or security-sensitive applications—would strengthen the ethical considerations.

Rating:  
5: Borderline accept – Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes to use a traditional intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) to assess the repeatability of speech embeddings learnt by neural network. The proposed ICC regularizer has characteristics similar to well-known contrastive loss which aims to minimize the intra-class variance and maximize the inter-class variance of training data. The authors propose to use the ICC regularizer not in isolation but in combination with the contrastive loss. This paper performs several types of experiments based on speech processing tasks such as speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection to demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed regularizer. These tasks focus on the importance of detecting the repeatability for the target data.
reviews.strengths:
- The considered scenario is interesting and important to the community. The proposed method is simple yet effective in assessing the repeatability in the target data.
- This paper assesses the effectiveness of the proposed method not only with a single speech task but also with several tasks based on voice quality evaluation. The improvements look sufficient.
reviews.weaknesses:
- ICC is very traditional metric, which is widely used for various data classification algorithms.
- The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation.
- Lack of some experiments and details of experimental setup (see questions below).
reviews.questions:
- It looks like that the proposed ICC regularizer corresponds to the special case of contrastive loss focused only on positive sample pairs. Although there are many variants in contrastive loss, the basic concept is to increase a capability of category classification of data by leveraging both positive and negative pairs. I am wondering if the training only with conventional contrastive loss could provide some improvements similar to the proposed method, for example, by simply changing the ratio of positive sample pairs (reducing negative sample pairs) in the last half of the training stage. What are advantages that cannot be obtained by just changing the ratio of positive and negative pair data?
- It is difficult to understand the effectiveness of the proposed method correctly because experimental setups including data are very briefly written. For example, in the experiment described in Section 4.1, what are the training strategy of the model such as an optimizer, a learning rate scheduling, other regularization techniques (specAugment, DropConnect, etc.), data augmentation, and so on?
- In addition, a summary of data used for each experiment (a few more details) would be very much helpful.
reviews.limitations: The same comments as one of weaknesses. The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation but be used in combination with a conventional contrastive loss as clearly mentioned in the paper.

Summary:
This paper proposes to use a traditional intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) to assess the repeatability of speech embeddings learnt by neural network. The proposed ICC regularizer has characteristics similar to well-known contrastive loss which aims to minimize the intra-class variance and maximize the inter-class variance of training data. The authors propose to use the ICC regularizer not in isolation but in combination with the contrastive loss. This paper performs several types of experiments based on speech processing tasks such as speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection to demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed regularizer. These tasks focus on the importance of detecting the repeatability for the target data.

Strengths:
- The considered scenario is interesting and important to the community. The proposed method is simple yet effective in assessing the repeatability in the target data.
- This paper assesses the effectiveness of the proposed method not only with a single speech task but also with several tasks based on voice quality evaluation. The improvements look sufficient.

Weaknesses:
- ICC is very traditional metric, which is widely used for various data classification algorithms.
- The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation.
- Lack of some experiments and details of experimental setup (see questions below).

Questions:
- It looks like that the proposed ICC regularizer corresponds to the special case of contrastive loss focused only on positive sample pairs. Although there are many variants in contrastive loss, the basic concept is to increase a capability of category classification of data by leveraging both positive and negative pairs. I am wondering if the training only with conventional contrastive loss could provide some improvements similar to the proposed method, for example, by simply changing the ratio of positive sample pairs (reducing negative sample pairs) in the last half of the training stage. What are advantages that cannot be obtained by just changing the ratio of positive and negative pair data?
- It is difficult to understand the effectiveness of the proposed method correctly because experimental setups including data are very briefly written. For example, in the experiment described in Section 4.1, what are the training strategy of the model such as an optimizer, a learning rate scheduling, other regularization techniques (specAugment, DropConnect, etc.), data augmentation, and so on?
- In addition, a summary of data used for each experiment (a few more details) would be very much helpful.

Limitations:
